<@UC6997THT> Thanks!

I think there are a lot of interesting questions about how (/ if) to work types into a system like this. I agree that they could help for choosing data visualizations. You could even go all the way to a Haskell-y, type-class-y thing. On the other hand, it's worth asking: Does PANE really need types? How often do these ambiguous cases occur? How much of a problem would it be to just ask the user to disambiguate when needed?

reminds me if anyone is doing <https://adventofcode.com>, it will be cool to marry these 2 ideas together :slightly_smiling_face:

Thanks <@UDJ2TKB99>, the Widdows et al. link is very interesting, and makes a good stab at one of the most difficult problems, of building ontologies retroactively which previously I'd only seen tackled by "Semprola", Oli Sharpe's system which I mentioned in the other thread. I'd like to get some more technical detail on how the "roles" system is implemented and interpreted in practice, and perhaps even some source code : P The "noms" project seems to concentrate on the less interesting part of the problem, that is, how can we assure the integrity and availability of the data, the problem subset that we see tackled, for example, in the current incarnation of the "Dat" project - <https://datproject.org/> . It's great that our case studies also focus on visualizing wildlife sightings, got a few links to those followup papers?

text:
Thanks for the kind words on the paper, <@UCUSW7WVD> - indeed as you suggest, a program addition operator for arbitrary code is not workable, under the "black box" model. This is why we propose the abolition of program code as currently constituted - you can check out the parallel paper at <https://github.com/amb26/papers/raw/master/onward-2016/onward-2016.pdf> on the "Open Authorial Principle" for positive and negative constraints on how this replacement for code will need to be organised. Indeed it must operate on the "white box" principles you suggest - also note section 5.4 which explains that our program algebra is not like others which have been proposed, in being primarily decompositional rather than compositional. The OAP I think offers helpful economics and models of how the adopting community can "mess with the guts" of adopted artefacts whilst i) maintaining a relationship with the upstream community, ii) not necessarily disrupting the upstream community's artefacts.
Indeed comprehensibility is a key virtue underpinning all authorial activities, an important prerequisite of which is visibility/addressibility.
I should stop saying "indeed" but my 2-yr-old son has recently adopted it as an idiom from his favorite TV character, Flop.

<@UEH6T3RJB> I've been doing my own literature review of the MAYA work to refresh my memories—I'm glad you're finding some ideas in there. As for source code/running systems, I've reached out to former coworkers to understand the current state of things and what I can direct people to or potentially share from my own archives. Here are a few papers with more details on "roles":

• Roles in the universal database: Data and metadata in a distributed semantic network: <https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Dominic_Widdows/publication/228938561_Roles_in_the_universal_database_Data_and_metadata_in_a_distributed_semantic_network/links/0912f509937ef0e153000000/Roles-in-the-universal-database-Data-and-metadata-in-a-distributed-semantic-network.pdf>
• Managing distributed collaboration in a peer-to-peer network: <https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Dominic_Widdows/publication/220830033_Managing_Distributed_Collaboration_in_a_Peer-to-Peer_Network/links/5481f3410cf2e5f7ceab10ff.pdf>
• Collaborative Annotation that Lasts Forever: Using Peer-to-Peer Technology for Disseminating Corpora and Language Resources: <https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228725378_Collaborative_annotation_that_lasts_forever_Using_peer-to-peer_technology_for_disseminating_corpora_and_language_resources/download>
• And here's a link to a figure in another paper depicting a wildlife usage (as I recall it was a limited test): <https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Visualization-of-sightings-of-the-Florida-Panther_fig3_242396923>

Fascinating discussion at that link, <@UCUSW7WVD>. Thanks for sharing. I wonder if you can solve the same problem (different subsystems producing/consuming slightly different sets of fields, and therefore all fields are optional) with subtyping and adaptors with defaults. My intuition is that you’re alway going to be better off if each subsystem publishes its I/O assumptions precisely (via input type declarations and output type declarations w/optional fields explicit) and then those differences are managed at an interface boundary. The reason for that intuition is that it’s always better to tell the truth.

<@UEH6T3RJB> - yes, all understanding is misunderstanding, if I misunderstand correctly? :smile: Perhaps all we can hope for is for communication to be compatible, but our internal cognition is...impossible to compare directly. Seems similar to how I don't really know if anyone else senses the color 'red' the exact same way as me. I'm looking forward to reading the book.

Hi everyone,

I'm Phil and I suffer from the same the disease that many of you do. I know, just know, that there could be a much better, much easier, much more powerful way to write software than the way we're doing it at the moment.
And I want to do something about it ... if only I could figure out what.
I muse and throw up random thoughts about this stuff both on my blog here : <http://sdi.thoughtstorms.info/> and in Quora answers, now aggregated here : <https://www.quora.com/q/qqylyzqgpwisoecc>

Thanks for the great links and feedback! I hadn't actually thought of the rationale you gave for restarts. It makes sense though. A far more tolerable, hence more common, microcosm of the "try reinstalling Windows" shrug-of-the-shoulders "solution". Too complex to reason about => destroy everything and start again from scratch, which is at least a well-understood state.

I've just added a short bit to the end I somehow didn't think to include, until a mention of "aggressively pushed updates" in the one of the above papers brought up some unpleasant memories and fire in my heart. *Now* it feels officially finished :slightly_smiling_face:

My first love language as an undergrad was Smalltalk. My weapon of choice today is Clojure. I generally like the stuff that I'm sure most people like : Lisps, Smalltalk, Parc, HARC.

My current thinking on new languages / areas of investigation / demands in my manifesto are these :

1) expressing computation is a solved problem. We have Lambda calculus, Lisp-like functions, Smalltalk-like messages. These are fine. We don't need to worry about these.

The areas we need to explore further are in expressing complex data-structures, "architecture" and constraints. My recent thinking on OO is that what makes OO "hard" is that we don't have good ways of expressing larger scale assemblages of objects. And there's too much focus on the individual class, rather than the collective of how classes or objects act together.

2) A second problem is that programming languages make us do the work in an order that suits them. Our job as programmers is to give a number of constraints to the computer that define a program. The problem is that the computer makes us give the constraints in the order and format that is convenient for it. Often requiring us to fit into architectural restrictions that we don't, in fact, care about but which are part of the fussy housekeeping of the system ... eg. why am I jumping between different files to fill in information about model, about view, about database, about client UI? Etc? Why can't I express what's important to me, in one place, in the order that suits me, and have the computer go back and wire these together?

The first of these thoughts is making me interested in grammars. And the way that ML or Haskell define complex data-schema with algebraic data-types in an almost Backus-Naur style. Can we use something like this to declare assemblages of objects? (See a discussion here : <https://www.quora.com/q/qqylyzqgpwisoecc/Whats-wrong-with-OO> )

The second of these thoughts has me looking into logic programming in Prolog and Minikanren where we "flatten" our program into a sequence of facts and rules which can be added in more or less any order, leaving the inference engine running around drawing out the implications. (See my discussion with Alexander Tchitchigin in the comments of this : <https://www.quora.com/What-is-the-programming-language-you-are-looking-for-and-why/answer/Phil-Jones> )
Two questions I thought about today: 1) is there any other data representation/query language that has a theoretical foundation other than SQL/relational algebra and datalog?
Two questions I thought about today: 1) is there any other data representation/query language that has a theoretical foundation other than SQL/relational algebra and datalog?
2) do you consider reduce/foldLeft to be "fundamental" at the level of map and filter? is there another way of expresing? why reduce never has a special syntax in languages?

Looking for comments, opinions on an idea I've been thinking about, described here : <http://sdi.thoughtstorms.info/?p=1344>

Something we could call "Assemblage Programming"

Does this make sense? Can anyone see anything wrong with this idea? Is there a language out there that actually works like this?

Finally, I'd like to build a prototype of this. But what would people recommend as the compile target? I'd quite like a very simple, prototype-based OO virtual machine. Maybe that is just Javascript. Or maybe something else. Anyone know of a good, high-level overview of what a Smalltalk or minimal OO VM architecture looks like?

The main difference between map and filter is that reduce is more likely to change the shape of the final output. The result of map and filter are still collections (though map obviously can change the type of what the collection contains)

You could perhaps argue that reduces like "sum" or "group by" in SQL *are* a kind of special syntax for for reduce.

What do you mean by "theoretical foundation"? Any query has some kind of model behind it. Even the tape in a Turing machine.
What do you mean by "theoretical foundation"? Any query has some kind of model behind it. Even the tape in a Turing machine.

In this podcast, you describe two of your influences: "Out of the Tar Pit", and Conal Elliott's "Can functional programming be liberated from the von Neumann paradigm?"

I read them both (thanks for the tip-offs!), and both in turn refer to Backus' 1977 Turing lecture "Can Programming Be Liberated from the von Neumann Style?".

This turns out to include an early (the earliest?) description of the approach that I'm using in Onex! Again, thanks for the indirect tip-off!

Backus called the approach AST or Applicative State Transition systems. Here, you have pure functions that transform a state into a new state that replaces it.

I'm guessing it wasn't very popular, as I have never come across it until today (AFAIR), and I've been researching this whole area since the 1980s! :slightly_smiling_face:

Out of the Tar pit, of course, proposes a different "FRP" - Functional Relational Programming, which is similar in its approach to state and state transformation by a pure logic.

that's a good observation, maybe there are many specializations to reduce that have syntax and reduce is too general (can do too many things to have a single/simple "representation")

sql is based on a formal model (relational algebra) that was defined before it and was not "contaminated" by implementation/optimization decisions, maybe that's why it lasted so long

Yes, Backus definitely deserves a hat tip from me, but I often forget him. Conal Elliot talks about Backus frequently. Conal also mentions Peter Landin a lot
